planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist, sheltered site.

directions: Plant the clump at soil level in cool, fertile, well-drained ground and keep it evenly moist. This distinctive South American clumper has solid culms clothed in dense bottlebrush tufts of foliage from the branch clusters. It resents heat and drought, so give it cool roots, steady water, and shelter from drying wind.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist, open site with room to grow.

directions: Plant the division at grade in deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water well to establish. This tall, elegant clumper sends up arching culms ringed with fine feathery branch clusters and slender leaves. Give it cool, damp roots and shelter from harsh sun and wind, thinning older canes to show the graceful form.

planting.note: Pot or repot in spring as new roots begin to push.

directions: Grow this white-flowered species in a coarse bark or sphagnum mix in a pot with generous drainage, keeping the crown above the medium. Provide bright, indirect light and steady warmth, and let the roots approach dryness between thorough waterings. Long arching sprays open in winter and spring and can last for many weeks.

planting.note: Repot after flowering as fresh growth resumes in spring.

directions: Set this silver-mottled species in an open bark mix and mount or pot it so the fleshy roots stay well aerated. Give bright, filtered light and warm days with a slight nighttime cool-down to encourage the branching, many-flowered sprays of soft pink blooms. Water freely in active growth and reduce slightly once the tall spikes emerge in late winter.

planting.note: Repot in spring using fresh bark when roots start to move.

directions: Pot this Philippine species in coarse bark and keep it consistently warm and humid, with bright shade rather than direct sun. Water thoroughly and allow the medium to dry only slightly between drinks. Branching spikes carry many white flowers spotted red on the lower petals, opening mainly in winter.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new root tips appear.

directions: Grow this warmth-loving species in a well-draining bark mix, keeping humidity high and airflow steady. Provide bright, indirect light and water when the roots begin to silver. Soft pink flowers appear on arching sprays, most often in autumn and early winter.

planting.note: Repot in spring; this species blooms young and often.

directions: This compact, free-flowering species does well in a small pot of fine bark or sphagnum kept lightly moist. Give bright, filtered light and warm, humid conditions year round. Branching sprays of small pink or white flowers can bloom almost continuously and often produce keikis along the old spikes.

planting.note: Repot in spring and keep very warm through summer.

directions: Provide this fragrant, heat-loving species with warm temperatures, high humidity, and bright shade in an open bark or sphagnum mix. Keep the roots evenly moist and never allow prolonged drying. Waxy, star-shaped flowers in rose-violet open a few at a time on short spikes that rebloom over several seasons.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and humid all year.

directions: Once treated as a form of P. violacea, this fragrant species wants steady warmth, high humidity, and bright filtered light. Grow it in coarse bark or on a mount and keep the roots consistently damp. Green-and-magenta star flowers with a powerful scent open in flushes on short, persistent spikes.

planting.note: Repot in spring; expect frequent keiki formation.

directions: Give this warm-growing species bright shade, high humidity, and an open bark mix kept lightly moist. Waxy, fragrant flowers barred and spotted in purple open a few at a time on short spikes. Plants readily form keikis, which can be potted up once they have several roots.

planting.note: Repot in spring and keep warm year round.

directions: This warm-loving species carries yellow flowers strongly barred in cinnamon-brown, opening successively on a slowly lengthening spike. Grow it in fine bark or sphagnum with bright shade and high humidity. Water when the medium approaches dryness and feed lightly during active growth.

planting.note: Repot in spring; the flattened spikes rebloom for years.

directions: Named for its flattened, antler-like flower stalk, this durable species blooms repeatedly from the same spikes, so leave them intact. Grow it warm and humid in bark or on a mount with bright, indirect light. Star-shaped yellow flowers barred in brown appear a few at a time over many months.

planting.note: Repot sparingly; this slow species dislikes root disturbance.

directions: Famous for enormous pendulous leaves that can span two feet or more, this slow-growing species needs warmth, humidity, and patience. Grow it in coarse bark with bright shade and disturb the roots as little as possible. Pendant sprays of round, cream flowers heavily spotted maroon appear only on well-established plants.

planting.note: Repot in spring using fresh open bark.

directions: This Philippine species bears waxy yellow flowers boldly banded with red-brown that open a few at a time and often rebloom from old spikes. Provide bright shade, warm temperatures, and steady humidity in an open bark mix. Let the roots dry slightly between waterings and feed lightly while growing.

planting.note: Repot in spring; tolerates a slightly cooler winter rest.

directions: A Himalayan-foothill species that accepts slightly cooler, intermediate conditions than most moth orchids. Grow it in bark or mounted, with bright shade and good airflow, and reduce watering somewhat in winter. Sprays of starry orange-and-brown flowers with a white lip open mainly in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring; give a warm, bright summer to bloom well.

directions: Formerly placed in Doritis, this terrestrial-leaning species has upright leaves and tall spikes of small purple to pink flowers. Grow it warm and bright in a fine, moisture-retentive bark or sphagnum mix. Summer warmth and good light drive the long-lasting, branching flower stalks.

planting.note: Repot in spring; spikes rebloom, so keep them.

directions: Named for the hieroglyph-like markings on its creamy flowers, this warm-growing Philippine species blooms successively over many months. Provide bright shade, high humidity, and an open bark mix kept lightly moist. Leave old spikes attached, as they flower again the following season.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and evenly moist.

directions: This warm, humidity-loving species is prized for white flowers randomly splashed with reddish-brown bars that vary from bloom to bloom. Grow it in coarse bark or on a mount with bright shade and keep the roots consistently damp. Flowers open a few at a time on short, reblooming spikes.

planting.note: Repot in spring; appreciates a slightly cooler night.

directions: A slender Philippine species with silver-marbled leaves and arching sprays of small pink flowers finely striped in darker rose. Give it bright shade, high humidity, and a slight nighttime cool-down. Grow in an open bark mix and water when the roots begin to silver.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring; give a drier winter rest.

directions: This miniature, deciduous species drops its leaves in winter and should be kept drier during that rest. Mount it or use a small pot of fine bark, with bright shade and warm, humid growing conditions. Short sprays of tiny white flowers with a bold purple lip open in spring as new growth resumes.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and humid year round.

directions: A compact species from Sulawesi with attractively silver-mottled leaves and arching sprays of small white flowers. Grow it warm and humid in fine bark or sphagnum, kept lightly moist and never bone dry. Provide bright shade and gentle air movement to keep the foliage healthy.

planting.note: Repot in spring using fresh open bark.

directions: This Sulawesi species bears waxy, fragrant flowers in yellow to orange-brown that darken as they age. Grow it warm with bright shade and steady humidity in an open bark mix. Flowers open successively on short spikes, so leave old stalks in place to rebloom.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and moist through summer.

directions: A warm-growing species with pendulous sprays of white to cream flowers heavily barred in reddish-brown. Give it high humidity, bright shade, and roots kept consistently damp in coarse bark or on a mount. Feed lightly during active growth and provide steady air movement.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and humid year round.

directions: A Borneo species valued for fragrant, waxy yellow flowers that open in succession on a lengthening spike. Grow it warm and humid with bright shade in an open bark mix kept lightly moist. Leave the spikes attached, as they continue to produce flowers over a long season.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep evenly warm and humid.

directions: A small Borneo species with fragrant white-to-pink flowers striped in rose-purple. Grow it warm and humid in fine bark or sphagnum with bright shade. Keep the medium lightly moist and provide gentle airflow around the compact plant.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and evenly moist.

directions: This Philippine species carries a few thick, waxy, ivory-white flowers on short spikes. Grow it warm and humid with bright shade in an open, fast-draining bark mix. Keep the roots lightly moist and avoid letting the plant dry out for long.

planting.note: Repot in spring; tolerates intermediate conditions.

directions: A compact species from Java and Sumatra with white to pale-pink flowers marked by a fringed lip. It accepts intermediate to warm conditions with bright shade and steady humidity. Grow in fine bark kept lightly moist and provide good air movement.

planting.note: Repot in spring; give a cooler, drier winter rest.

directions: A Chinese species from Yunnan that tolerates cooler, more seasonal conditions than tropical moth orchids. Give it bright shade in summer and a drier, cooler rest in winter. Grow in fine bark or mounted, with sprays of pale yellow-green flowers opening in the warm season.

planting.note: Mount in spring; give a cool, dry, leafless winter rest.

directions: This cool-tolerant Chinese species drops most of its leaves and rests dry through winter, relying on green photosynthetic roots. Mount it or use a small pot of fine bark, keeping it cool and nearly dry until spring. Fragrant pink flowers open on short spikes as growth resumes.

planting.note: Mount in spring; give a drier winter rest.

directions: A charming miniature from the Himalayan foothills that loses its leaves and rests drier in winter. Mount it or grow in a small pot of fine bark with bright shade and intermediate warmth. Small white flowers with a bold red-brown-marked lip open in spring.

planting.note: Mount in spring; tolerates cool winters if kept drier.

directions: Once placed in Sedirea, this cool-tolerant East Asian miniature is one of the hardiest moth orchids in cultivation. Mount it or grow in fine bark with bright shade and a cool, drier winter. Fragrant white flowers spotted and barred in purple open on short arching spikes in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm with bright shade.

directions: Closely allied to P. schilleriana, this species has beautifully silver-mottled leaves and branching sprays of white flowers with a yellow, red-spotted lip. Grow it warm and humid with bright, filtered light. Water freely in active growth and ease off once the tall spikes develop.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and humid year round.

directions: A Borneo species named for the leopard-like spotting on its yellow-green flowers. Grow it warm and humid with bright shade in an open bark mix kept lightly moist. Flowers open successively on short spikes, which should be left in place to rebloom.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm and evenly moist.

directions: A fragrant Borneo species with pale flowers striped and barred in reddish-brown and a rose-colored lip. Give it warm temperatures, high humidity, and roots kept consistently damp in coarse bark or on a mount. Provide bright shade and steady air movement for best growth.

planting.note: Repot in spring; keep warm with bright shade.

directions: This widespread Southeast Asian species bears waxy, fragrant cream flowers barred in red-brown on short spikes. Grow it warm and humid with bright shade in an open bark mix kept lightly moist. Leave old spikes attached, as they flower again over multiple seasons.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring; keep humid and shaded.

directions: A tiny species from Indochina that appreciates lower light and constant humidity. Grow it mounted or in a small pot of fine bark, keeping the roots lightly moist and never fully dry. Small white flowers flushed pink open on short spikes over the warm season.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring; keep warm and humid.

directions: Formerly placed in Kingidium, this small, wide-ranging species has wavy-edged leaves and short spikes of tiny white-to-lavender flowers. Grow it warm and humid with bright shade in fine bark or mounted. Keep the roots lightly moist and provide gentle air movement.

planting.note: Mount in spring; give a cool, drier winter rest.

directions: A cool-tolerant, near-leafless miniature from the Himalayas and southern China that rests dry in winter. Mount it or use a small pot of fine bark, keeping it cool and nearly dry during dormancy. Small greenish-yellow flowers marked in purple open on short spikes in the growing season.

planting.note: Repot or divide as new roots begin to push in spring.

directions: Pot the pseudobulbs in coarse bark or a mounted slab, setting the rhizome at the surface with the newest growth facing out for room to expand. Give bright filtered light, let the mix approach dryness between soakings, and feed a dilute balanced fertilizer during active growth. Large ruffled lavender blooms open in autumn once the leading sheath matures.

planting.note: Divide clumps just after flowering as fresh roots appear.

directions: Anchor the rhizome on coarse bark with the growing tip clear of the pot edge, keeping roots aerated. Provide warm days, bright shade, and a cooler dry rest in winter to set buds. Spring flowers in soft rose with a frilled, veined lip give this Venezuelan species its Easter-season reputation.

planting.note: Repot in spring when new roots start extending.

directions: Grow the pseudobulbs in well-draining bark and let them dry noticeably between waterings. Bright light and a distinct winter cool-down encourage the winter bloom cycle. The pale lilac flowers with a boldly colored lip open around the holidays, making this Colombian native a classic gift orchid.

planting.note: Best divided as growth resumes in spring.

directions: Give this large Colombian species a roomy bark-filled pot and stake the tall pseudobulbs as they develop. Water freely during the summer growth flush, then ease off after the leaves harden. Enormous fragrant lavender flowers with twin yellow lip-eyes appear in summer.

planting.note: Handle carefully and repot only as new roots emerge.

directions: Mount or pot in fast-draining bark and keep warm year round, as this species resents cold and soggy roots. Bright light brings out the golden petals set against a crimson, gold-veined lip. Summer flowers are richly fragrant; give a light, drier rest after blooming.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping at least three pseudobulbs per piece.

directions: This adaptable Brazilian bifoliate grows well in bark or on a mount and tolerates a range of conditions. Provide bright light, steady moisture in summer, and slightly drier winters. Clusters of star-shaped pale pink flowers with a tipped purple lip open in late spring.

planting.note: Prefers mounting; disturb roots as little as possible.

directions: A compact species that thrives mounted on cork or in a small shallow pot with excellent drainage. Its wandering rhizomes send flower spikes from separate short growths, so avoid burying the plant. Large fragrant rose-lavender blooms appear in winter and spring on this beloved miniature.

planting.note: Divide in spring as roots begin to grow.

directions: An easy, forgiving bifoliate for bark culture that appreciates strong light and good air movement. Water regularly through the growing season and reduce slightly in cooler months. Fragrant clusters of soft rosy-lilac flowers open in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Repot in spring using a fast-draining epiphyte mix.

directions: This wide-ranging Andean species tolerates warm lowland or cooler highland culture in bright light. Keep roots evenly moist during growth and just barely damp in winter. Arching sprays of soft lilac flowers marked with a fan of purple veins open mostly in fall and early winter.

planting.note: Divide after winter bloom as new growth starts.

directions: A compact, heat-tolerant species from Venezuela that grows readily in bark with bright light and warmth. Allow a slightly drier winter rest to trigger flowering. Fragrant lavender blooms with a deeply colored, gold-throated lip open in winter around the holidays.

planting.note: Repot when new roots emerge, typically spring to early summer.

directions: Grow this warm-loving Venezuelan species in coarse bark and give it strong light for best flowering. Water well in growth and reduce during the short winter rest. Large fragrant rose-pink flowers with a broad, ruffled purple lip open in spring.

planting.note: Divide as spring roots begin to extend.

directions: A summer-blooming unifoliate that tolerates warmth well and grows easily in bark culture. Provide bright light, generous water during growth, and only a light winter reduction. Fragrant soft-lavender flowers with a frilled lip open in early summer.

planting.note: Repot right after flowering as new roots start.

directions: This Colombian species enjoys bright light and a well-drained bark mix with a cool, drier winter rest. Water freely once new growth and roots are active. Spring flowers show delicate blush-white petals above a vivid crimson-and-gold lip.

planting.note: Give a tall pot; divide only when crowded.

directions: This tall Brazilian bifoliate builds cane-like pseudobulbs and benefits from staking and strong light. Water and feed steadily through the long growing season. Clusters of many pale pink, purple-spotted flowers with a rich amethyst lip open in spring.

planting.note: Best mounted so the sparse roots dry quickly.

directions: A small, sun-loving Brazilian species best grown mounted on cork with fast drying between waterings. Give strong light and good airflow to keep the short pseudobulbs firm. Waxy greenish flowers heavily barred with chocolate and a magenta lip open in summer.

planting.note: Divide in spring; also grows well mounted.

directions: An easygoing coastal Brazilian bifoliate that adapts to bright light and warm to intermediate temperatures. Keep evenly moist during growth and provide good air movement. Small greenish-tan flowers with a pale, red-lined tubular lip open in late summer.

planting.note: Use a tall pot to support the long pseudobulbs.

directions: This tall Brazilian bifoliate needs strong light, staking, and steady feeding through its long growth cycle. Water well in the growing months and give brighter, slightly drier conditions in winter. Waxy coppery-green flowers with a narrow, lipless-looking rosy tongue appear in fall.

planting.note: Give a large, deep pot for the tall canes.

directions: A vigorous, tall bifoliate that rewards strong light, ample water, and regular feeding during growth. Support the reedy pseudobulbs and allow a brief winter rest. Compact heads of olive-green, red-spotted flowers with a bright magenta lip open in fall.

planting.note: Grows best mounted or in a small, open basket.

directions: A compact, showy bifoliate that dislikes stale roots, so mount it or use a very open mix. Provide bright light and let it dry between waterings, with a drier winter rest. Waxy dark-spotted bronze flowers set off a large, veined magenta lip in summer.

planting.note: Mount or use small pots and keep cool and humid.

directions: Once known as Sophronitis coccinea, this cool-growing miniature wants constant humidity, gentle air movement, and never fully dry roots. Grow mounted or in a tiny pot with bright but not harsh light. Brilliant scarlet flowers, large for the plant, open in fall and winter.

planting.note: Repot after flowering; also grows on slabs or trees.

directions: The national flower of Costa Rica, this cluster-flowered species grows easily in bark or mounted in bright light. Water freely in growth and give a light, drier rest before spring bloom. Rounded heads of rosy-purple flowers with a white throat open in spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring; enjoys bright, warm conditions.

directions: This Central American species takes strong light and warmth and blooms well as a compact plant in bark. Keep it moist during growth and a bit drier once the pseudobulbs mature. Clusters of small, glowing orange to red flowers open in spring and are a key parent of many bright hybrids.

planting.note: Give a deep pot; it grows tall and clumps readily.

directions: A robust, water-loving species that grows near streams in the wild and appreciates steady moisture in cultivation. Give bright light and generous feeding during the strong summer growth. Tall spikes carry large clusters of rosy-purple flowers with a dark, white-eyed lip in autumn.

planting.note: Repot as roots emerge; tolerates cool coastal conditions.

directions: Formerly Laelia purpurata and the emblem of southern Brazil, this large orchid takes bright light and cooler nights than most cattleyas. Water well in growth and give strong airflow to keep the tall pseudobulbs firm. Big spring flowers show pale petals framing a deep purple, trumpet-shaped lip.

planting.note: Very tolerant; mount or pot and grow cool and bright.

directions: One of the hardiest Cattleya-alliance orchids, this Mexican species shrugs off cool nights and brief chills near freezing. Give strong light, dry it out between waterings, and rest it in winter. Long, wiry spikes lift lavender-pink flowers with a purple, gold-marked lip in early winter.

planting.note: Best mounted; needs a hard, dry winter rest.

directions: This drought-adapted Central American species grows on sunny branches and needs bright light with sharp drainage. Water freely only in the growing season, then keep nearly dry through the winter. Slender spikes bear soft pink to white flowers with a maroon-blotched throat in late fall and winter.

planting.note: Grow cool and bright with a distinct winter rest.

directions: A hardy Mexican species long grown in gardens there, valued for cool tolerance and reliable winter bloom. Give strong light and water well in growth, then keep dry and bright once the pseudobulbs ripen. Tall spikes carry rich rose-purple flowers around the winter holidays.

planting.note: Needs a long, dry, bright winter rest to thrive.

directions: This compact highland Mexican species grows on oak branches and demands very bright light and a long dry rest. Water only during the summer growth flush and mount it so roots dry fast. Unusually large soft-lavender flowers, big for the small plant, open in late spring.

planting.note: Give strong light and repot only when necessary.

directions: The national flower of Honduras carries blue-green, waxy leaves and needs very bright light and sharp drainage. Water well in growth and reduce afterward, allowing the mix to dry. A single large pale-green flower with a deeply fringed lip opens in summer and is fragrant at night.

planting.note: Mount or pot in a fast-draining, bright spot.

directions: A compact relative of digbyana with glaucous foliage, wanting bright light and excellent drainage. Water during active growth and keep drier through the resting months. Solitary greenish to pale flowers with a broad, unfringed lip open in spring.

planting.note: Grows best mounted with roots left exposed to air.

directions: This terete-leaved epiphyte grows well mounted where its pencil-like foliage and roots dry quickly. Give bright light, water regularly in growth, and mist during dry spells. Long, spidery cream to greenish flowers with narrow tails open in summer and fall and are fragrant at night.

planting.note: Easy in bark; divide when the pot fills.

directions: The national flower of Belize is one of the easiest orchids, thriving in bark with bright shade and steady moisture. Keep it evenly watered and lightly fed year round, easing off only slightly in winter. Its upside-down flowers show a dark, shell-shaped hood over pale, striped petals over a long season.

planting.note: Give strong light and a drier winter rest.

directions: A robust, sun-loving epiphyte that grows in bark or mounted with strong light and good air movement. Water freely during growth, then keep noticeably drier and bright over winter to set flowers. Fragrant spring sprays carry chocolate-brown petals above a broad rosy-pink lip.

planting.note: Divide in spring; grows readily in bark.

directions: This popular cluster-type hybrid (C. guttata x C. aurantiaca) is vigorous and takes strong light and warmth. Water and feed steadily during the growing season and give good airflow. Rounded heads of glossy deep red to mahogany flowers open in summer on this widely grown classic.

planting.note: Repot in spring using a fast-draining bark mix.

directions: A compact, free-flowering red hybrid, with the cultivar 'Scheherazade' especially prized for its intense scarlet color. Grow in bark with bright light, steady water in growth, and a slight winter reduction. Rounded, waxy red flowers open in winter and last well.

planting.note: Divide as new roots start in spring.

directions: A landmark white hybrid famous for its large, rounded, fragrant blooms and full form. Grow in bark with bright light, water well during growth, and give a light winter rest. Pure white flowers with a faint yellow throat open in fall and winter.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring as new roots begin to push from the base.

directions: Pot in a fast-draining bark mix and set canes so the base sits just above the surface. Give bright filtered light with a warm summer growing season, then reduce water once the canes finish maturing to trigger the purple autumn spikes. Sprays of magenta flowers open along tall arching stems and last for weeks.

planting.note: Split crowded clumps in spring, keeping several canes per division.

directions: Grow this tough Australian rock orchid in a shallow pot of coarse bark or on a mounted slab. Allow a cool, dryish rest through winter to set the fragrant pale-pink spring flowers. Baby plantlets often form along old canes and can be potted up once they root.

planting.note: Repot just as spring growth resumes so new roots settle quickly.

directions: Set the club-shaped pseudobulbs in a well-drained bark mix and grow warm and bright while in active growth. Give a distinctly cooler, drier winter rest to encourage the arching sprays of golden yellow flowers. Blooms appear from the tops of mature canes in spring and carry a light sweet scent.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring as fresh growth begins at the cane base.

directions: Grow the long pendulous canes on a mount or in a hanging basket so they can drape freely. Water and feed heavily through the summer, then stop water and let the canes drop their leaves for a dry winter rest. Fragrant lavender flowers burst from the leafless canes in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Best mounted in spring so the small pseudobulbs stay well drained.

directions: This compact species does best mounted on cork or bark where its ridged pseudobulbs dry quickly between waterings. Keep it warm and bright in summer, then give a cool dry winter to set bloom. Short cascading sprays of honey-yellow flowers appear from the base of the bulbs in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring only when the mix breaks down, as it resents disturbance.

directions: Grow the four-angled canes in a bark mix with strong light and steady summer feeding. A cool, brighter, drier winter rest is essential for the dense pineapple-like flower trusses. Pendulous clusters of golden yellow blooms hang from near the cane tops in spring.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring as new roots appear at the cane base.

directions: Grow these stubby deciduous canes on a mount or in a small pot of open bark. Water generously in summer, then keep dry and cool once the leaves yellow and drop. Fragrant amethyst flowers with dark eye spots open along the bare canes in spring.

planting.note: Best mounted in spring so the sprawling thin canes can trail.

directions: A miniature species that grows into a tangled mat of slender canes, ideal for a cork mount. Water freely during warm growth and give a cool, slightly dry winter rest. Each cane produces small rose-pink flowers with a fringed orange-centered lip in spring.

planting.note: Divide large clumps in spring, keeping plenty of pseudobulbs together.

directions: This robust Australian rock lily grows on coarse bark, rock, or a large well-drained pot and tolerates cool nights. Grow it bright and let it dry somewhat in winter to build the long flower racemes. Established plants send up dense arching spikes of creamy-white to yellow scented flowers in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new growth and roots emerge from the base.

directions: Give this tall grower a sturdy pot of bark and strong light with generous summer feeding. Reduce water and cool it slightly in winter without a hard dry rest. Pendulous sprays of soft apricot-yellow flowers with a pouched, hairy lip open in late spring.

planting.note: Pot up in spring so roots establish before the summer growth push.

directions: Grow the tall canes bright and warm with heavy watering and feeding all summer. Ease off water and give cooler nights in winter to ripen the canes for flowering. Clusters of rich golden-orange flowers with a deeply fringed lip hang from near the cane tips in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring only when necessary, as it dislikes root disturbance.

directions: Grow the ribbed canes in open bark with bright light and steady summer moisture. A cooler, brighter, drier winter rest brings on the showy flower trusses. Dense hanging clusters pair pure white petals with a fuzzy golden-orange lip in spring.

planting.note: Repot into fresh live sphagnum in spring to keep roots cool and fresh.

directions: This cool-growing miniature needs constant humidity, gentle air movement, and steady cool temperatures with no dry rest. Pot it in fine live sphagnum kept evenly damp but never soggy. Disproportionately large red, orange, or pink flowers can persist for several months on the tiny plant.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new roots begin extending from the pseudobulbs.

directions: Grow the angled pseudobulbs in open bark, warm and bright, with generous summer water. A cool, drier winter rest sets the pendulous flower trusses. Soft pink flowers with a yellow-blushed lip hang in graceful clusters in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring and keep warm year-round with only light seasonal change.

directions: This warm-growing New Guinea species wants steady heat, humidity, and light watering all year with no dry rest. Pot it in coarse, freely draining bark and feed lightly through the growing season. Bizarrely twisted cream-and-maroon flowers open in arching sprays and last for weeks.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring as fresh growth resumes at the cane base.

directions: Grow the pendulous deciduous canes on a mount or in a hanging basket of open mix. Water well in summer, then withhold water and cool the plant once the leaves fall. Large white flowers tipped in magenta with a dark-eyed yellow throat open along the bare canes in late winter.

planting.note: Repot in spring and avoid a hard winter dry rest for this group.

directions: Grow the black-haired canes in open bark, bright and warm, keeping them just barely moist through the cooler months rather than bone dry. Large crisp white flowers with a yellow throat open near the cane tops. Blooms are long-lasting and often carry a faint fresh fragrance.

planting.note: Repot in spring as the new canes and roots begin to grow.

directions: Grow the upright canes bright and warm with steady summer watering, then ease off in a cooler winter to set bloom. Dense one-sided flower spikes crowd tiny pink-purple blooms with orange tips into a brush-like shape. Give bright light for the best flowering.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring and keep cool and humid year-round.

directions: This cool highland species from the Philippines wants steady moisture, humidity, and cool nights with no dry rest. Grow it on a mount or in open mix so the pendulous canes can hang. Prized for unusually blue-and-white striped flowers that open along the older canes.

planting.note: Repot in spring and keep warm and evenly watered all year.

directions: A warm-growing New Guinea species that flowers repeatedly when kept hot, humid, and lightly watered year-round. Pot in coarse bark with excellent drainage and bright light. Flowers pair a white lip with upright green twisted petals that curl like antelope horns.

planting.note: Mount in spring so the fuzzy canes dry quickly after watering.

directions: This miniature is covered in white hairs and prefers a mount where its short canes dry fast. Water sparingly and give it a warm, bright spot with a dry winter rest. Waxy lemon-yellow flowers with a green-marked throat open from the fuzzy canes in spring.

planting.note: Mount or pot in spring and give a hard dry winter rest.

directions: Grow this small deciduous species mounted or in a shallow pot with sharp drainage. Water well in summer, then keep it dry and cool through winter once the leaves drop. Striking orange flowers held upside down, with a scent of citrus, open from the leafless canes in spring.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new growth starts at the base of the canes.

directions: This popular compact hybrid grows into a clump of short canes topped with fragrant flower sprays. Grow it warm and bright with even watering during active growth and slightly cooler nights to encourage bloom. Rounded flowers in berry-pink to purple open in dense clusters and carry a sweet scent.

planting.note: Divide crowded plants in spring as new growth begins.

directions: Grow this rambling species in a shallow pot or basket of open bark, or let it wander on a mount. Keep it bright and evenly moist while growing, easing off slightly in winter. Branching sprays carry many small bright-yellow flowers with a large flaring lip that dance in the slightest breeze.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring just as new roots appear.

directions: Pot the pseudobulbs in fine, freely draining bark and grow in bright light with even watering during growth. Reduce water once the pseudobulbs mature to help set the tall autumn flower spikes. Long branching sprays are crowded with golden-yellow flowers dominated by a large ruffled lip.

planting.note: Divide or repot in spring, keeping several pseudobulbs per piece.

directions: This famous hybrid grows in a pot of medium bark with bright light and even moisture during active growth. Let it dry a bit between waterings and feed regularly through the growing season. Tall branching spikes carry masses of maroon-and-white flowers with a strong chocolate-vanilla fragrance.

planting.note: Repot this compact hybrid in spring as new growth begins.

directions: A miniature hybrid perfect for a small pot of fine bark on a bright windowsill. Keep it evenly moist during growth and give it good light and humidity. Sprays of many tiny fragrant flowers in white, pink, or red appear in fall and winter and are wonderfully scented.

planting.note: Divide or repot in spring as new roots start to grow.

directions: This widely grown cut-flower hybrid wants bright light, medium bark, and even watering during active growth. Feed regularly and let the mix approach dryness between waterings. Tall arching sprays are packed with cheerful bright-yellow flowers marked with a chestnut-brown accent at the center.

planting.note: Repot in spring, keeping this small grower snug in its pot.

directions: Grow this compact species in a small pot of fine bark, bright and evenly moist while growing. It appreciates intermediate to cool nights and good humidity. Short, densely packed sprays of tiny golden-yellow flowers open in autumn with a sweet spicy fragrance.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring as new growth resumes.

directions: Grow the flat pseudobulbs in open bark with bright light and intermediate to cool nights. Water evenly during growth, then reduce slightly through the cooler months. Arching branched spikes carry waxy yellow-green flowers heavily blotched with chestnut-brown.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new roots appear, in a well-drained mix.

directions: This vigorous species produces very long, branching flower spikes and needs room and bright light. Pot in open bark, water evenly during growth, and give intermediate to cool nights. Countless small green-and-brown barred flowers with a clean white lip open over many weeks.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring just as new growth starts.

directions: Grow this Mexican species in open bark with bright light and cool to intermediate nights. Water evenly through the growing season and reduce moisture once the pseudobulbs mature. Branched spikes carry fragrant flowers with brown-barred yellow segments and a broad bright-yellow lip in autumn.

planting.note: Repot in spring as new growth and roots emerge.

directions: Pot this Brazilian species in medium bark and grow it bright with even watering during active growth. Ease off water once the pseudobulbs finish and give good air movement. Arching sprays carry rounded chestnut-brown flowers edged and marked in golden yellow with a frilled lip.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring just as new growth begins to push.

directions: Pot divisions of at least three growths into a fine bark mix with the rhizome sitting level with the surface. Keep the medium evenly damp but never soggy and provide cool nights in autumn to set buds. A single glossy bloom opens per stem in winter and can last two months.

planting.note: Repot mottled-leaf divisions in spring as roots start to move.

directions: Settle divisions into a moisture-retentive bark and perlite blend, keeping the crown clear of the medium to prevent rot. Grow warm and shaded year round and water once the surface dries slightly. The green-and-maroon flower with a dark pouch appears in spring on an upright stem.

planting.note: Repot in spring using a slightly limestone-enriched mix.

directions: Plant divisions in a fine, free-draining bark mix with a little crushed shell added for the alkalinity this species enjoys. Keep it warm and lightly shaded and reduce watering slightly in winter. Soft pink fragrant flowers open in spring above the silver-mottled foliage.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring after flowering finishes.

directions: Pot healthy divisions into medium bark, keeping the base of the fan firm but not buried. Grow in intermediate temperatures with steady humidity and even moisture at the roots. Large glossy tan-and-brown flowers with a lacquered sheen open in winter.

planting.note: Repot in spring as fresh roots emerge.

directions: Set divisions into a fine bark and perlite mix, keeping the rhizome at soil level. Provide warm, shaded, humid conditions and let the medium approach dryness between waterings. The white dorsal sepal with its bold purple stripe unfurls on autumn-to-winter stems.

planting.note: Divide mottled-leaf plants in spring after the winter bloom.

directions: Pot divisions in a moisture-retentive fine bark mix with the crown proud of the surface. Grow warm and shaded and keep humidity high with steady root moisture. Its heavily veined green-and-bronze flower with a netted pouch opens in winter.

planting.note: Repot divisions in spring into fresh warm-growing mix.

directions: Plant divisions into fine bark with the tessellated leaf fan sitting at the surface. Keep the plant warm, humid, and lightly shaded, watering when the mix nears dryness. Purple-and-white flowers with a whiskered petal edge appear in spring and summer.

planting.note: Repot in spring in a limestone-amended free-draining mix.

directions: Use a very open bark mix with added crushed shell or limestone chips for this calcium-loving species. Keep it warm and shaded with careful watering, as the low-set flower is prone to rot if wetted. The rounded white bloom heavily spotted maroon nestles just above the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Repot this slow grower in spring, disturbing the roots as little as possible.

directions: Grow this large, slow species in a coarse bark mix with excellent drainage and give it steady warmth and humidity. Water regularly and feed lightly, but expect several years before a fan is mature enough to spike. The tall stem carries multiple striped, horizontally held flowers in spring.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring once flowering has finished.

directions: This durable primary hybrid grows well in fine bark on a shaded windowsill, with the mottled leaf fan set at the surface. Keep it warm year round and water when the top of the mix begins to dry. Crisp green-and-white or dark art-shade flowers rise on tall stems and last for weeks.

planting.note: Repot in spring into a cool, moisture-retentive mix.

directions: Pot divisions into fine bark with a little limestone and keep the crown at the surface. Grow intermediate to cool with good humidity and steady moisture at the roots. The striking white flower veined violet with distinctive upswept petals opens in autumn.

planting.note: Repot in spring using a limestone-enriched, sharply draining mix.

directions: Grow this small calcium-lover in an open bark mix with crushed shell added and keep the low crown well clear of moisture. Provide warmth, humidity, and shade, watering carefully to avoid crown rot. Pale yellow flowers finely dotted with purple appear near the foliage, often several per season.

planting.note: Divide and repot in spring as new roots appear.

directions: Pot divisions into medium bark with the fan set at the surface and grow in intermediate, humid conditions. Water when the mix nears dryness and keep light moderate to bright-shaded. The large hairy stem carries a green-and-purple flower with wavy, spotted petals in spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring, keeping several bulbs per piece.

directions: Pot divisions into a coarse, free-draining terrestrial orchid mix with the bulbs half exposed. Grow in bright shade, keep evenly moist in the growing season, and give cool nights to encourage flowering. Upright spikes of small fragrant green-to-tan flowers appear from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide after flowering in early spring before new growth extends.

directions: Grow this cold-tolerant terrestrial in a gritty, well-drained mix and give it a cold winter rest to trigger buds. Keep it shaded and evenly moist during active growth and much drier when dormant. A single fragrant green or brown flower opens low on each short stem in late winter or early spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring as new growth begins.

directions: Pot into a coarse bark-based cymbidium mix with the bulbs sitting at the surface and grow in bright, cool conditions. Water freely in summer and keep humidity up, easing off in winter. Dense arching spikes of small red-and-yellow flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Divide after bloom in spring, keeping backbulbs attached.

directions: Give this large species a big pot of coarse bark mix, abundant summer water and feed, and bright light with cool autumn nights. Reduce watering after the growths mature to help set spikes. Long arching stems of big fragrant green-brown striped flowers open in late autumn and winter.

planting.note: Repot and divide in spring after flowering.

directions: Grow in a large container of coarse cymbidium mix with strong light and generous summer watering and feeding. Cool nights in late summer and autumn are essential to initiate the flower spikes. Long arching stems carry many green flowers with a red-marked lip in spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring and pot shallowly to accommodate pendent spikes.

directions: Pot this broad-leaved species in coarse mix, ideally in a basket so its hanging flower stems can drape freely. Grow in bright shade with steady moisture in summer and cooler, drier winters. Pendulous spikes of many olive-and-maroon flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring after the flowers fade.

directions: Grow in a coarse, well-drained mix with bright light and even moisture through the growing season. Give slightly cooler, drier conditions in winter to firm the growths. Fragrant ivory-white flowers with a yellow-marked lip open one or two per stem in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Divide in early spring while dormant, keeping clumps intact where possible.

directions: Pot this cool-loving terrestrial in a gritty, sharply draining mix and shelter it from hard freezes and hot sun. Keep evenly moist in summer and give a cool winter rest to set buds. Slender stems bear several narrow, very fragrant green flowers in late autumn and winter.

planting.note: Divide in early spring before the new season's growth extends.

directions: Grow this hardy terrestrial in a coarse, free-draining mix and give it a genuine cold winter to trigger flowering. Keep it shaded and evenly moist during growth and drier when at rest. Erect stems carry many small fragrant green flowers in spring.

planting.note: Take a top cutting with aerial roots in spring and mount or basket it.

directions: Grow this monopodial orchid bare-rooted in a slatted basket so its thick roots hang free, or mount it and water daily in warm weather. Give very bright light, high humidity, and good air movement, feeding regularly in growth. Long sprays of tessellated blue flowers open mostly in autumn.

planting.note: Root a stem cutting with several aerial roots in spring.

directions: Grow bare-rooted in a basket with the roots free to breathe, watering daily during warm spells and less in cool weather. Provide strong light, warmth, and steady feeding through the growing season. Fragrant checkered greenish-brown flowers with a blue-violet lip open through the warmer months.

planting.note: Take a top cutting bearing aerial roots in spring and basket it.

directions: Grow this warm-loving species bare-rooted in a basket with heavy humidity, daily watering, and very bright light. Keep temperatures consistently warm and feed frequently during active growth. Large flat rose-pink flowers with a tessellated lower half open on autumn spikes.

planting.note: Root a stem cutting with aerial roots in spring.

directions: Grow this smaller vanda bare-rooted in a basket with bright light, warmth, and frequent watering when in growth. Allow the roots to dry between waterings and keep humidity high. Arching sprays of pale lavender-blue flowers with a darker lip open in winter and spring.

planting.note: Root a stem cutting with aerial roots in warm weather.

directions: This scrambling hybrid grows readily from stem cuttings staked in a very free-draining bed or basket in full sun. Keep it warm and well watered with regular feeding and let it climb on supports. Slender pink-and-mauve flowers appear almost year round in tropical conditions.

planting.note: Take a top cutting with aerial roots in spring.

directions: Grow bare-rooted in a basket with strong light, warmth, and daily watering during the growing season. Give a slightly drier, cooler rest in winter to encourage flowering. Waxy, night-fragrant creamy-yellow flowers open on short sprays in spring.

planting.note: Root a stem cutting with aerial roots in spring.

directions: Grow this compact Himalayan species in a small basket or mount with intermediate to cool temperatures and bright light. Water freely in summer and reduce in winter, keeping humidity up. Long-lasting waxy green flowers with a striped, forked lip open in spring.

planting.note: Divide after flowering in spring, keeping at least three bulbs per piece.

directions: Pot divisions into a fine, moisture-retentive bark mix and never let this cool-growing orchid dry out completely. Give shaded light, steady humidity, and intermediate to cool temperatures. Flat, flower-flushed pink-and-white blooms with a bold face mask open mainly in late spring.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping the fine roots intact.

directions: Grow in a fine bark and moss mix kept evenly and constantly moist, as the thin roots resent drying out. Provide shade, high humidity, and cool to intermediate temperatures. White flowers with purple petal blotches and a yellow-marked lip open in spring and summer.

planting.note: Divide after bloom in spring, keeping several bulbs together.

directions: Pot this small species in fine bark or moss and keep it constantly moist in shaded, humid, cool conditions. Avoid mineral build-up by flushing with clean water regularly. White flowers with a boldly red-streaked lip appear several times a year.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping the delicate roots undisturbed.

directions: Grow in a fine, moisture-retentive mix kept evenly damp and never allowed to dry fully. Provide shade, steady humidity, and cool to intermediate temperatures. Small fragrant white flowers with a pale yellow lip mark open in spring and summer.

planting.note: Divide after flowering in spring, keeping several bulbs per piece.

directions: Pot into a fine bark and moss mix that stays evenly moist, and grow in shade with high humidity and cool nights. Flush regularly to keep salts from harming the fine roots. Ruffled rose-and-white flowers with a dark, complex lip pattern open in spring and summer.

planting.note: Set pseudobulbs out once hard frosts have passed and the soil has begun to warm.

directions: Plant the small pseudobulbs about one to two inches deep in loose, humus-rich soil that drains freely. Keep evenly moist through the growing season and reduce watering after the pleated leaves yellow in autumn. Slender spikes of pale pink to lilac flowers open in late spring above the fresh foliage.

planting.note: Divide and replant clumps as new growth begins in spring.

directions: Bury the corm-like pseudobulbs an inch or two below the surface in a bed enriched with leaf mold and grit. Water regularly while in leaf and give a sheltered spot to protect the arching flower spikes. Creamy yellow blooms marked with maroon appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant pseudobulbs in early spring for bloom the same season.

directions: Settle the pseudobulbs one to two inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground and mulch lightly in colder zones. This selection is prized for its rich, saturated purple flowers held on strong stems in late spring. Keep the clump moist during growth and let it dry somewhat once foliage dies back.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the ground is workable and frost risk is easing.

directions: This vigorous hybrid takes to a humus-rich, free-draining border with dappled shade. Plant pseudobulbs shallowly and water through the season, easing off as the pleated leaves fade. Apricot-flushed rose flowers with a marbled lip open on branching spikes in early summer.

planting.note: Set out pseudobulbs in early spring in a sheltered, partly shaded bed.

directions: Plant the pseudobulbs an inch or two deep in loose, gritty, organic soil. This form is grown for its unusually cool violet-blue toned flowers that open along the spike in late spring. Water steadily during the growing season and protect the crown with mulch where winters are severe.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn so roots establish before winter chill.

directions: Set the rhizome just below the surface with the growth buds pointing up in a neutral to slightly alkaline, moisture-retentive but airy soil. This prairie-fen species wants full sun to light shade and consistent moisture in spring. Compact white pouches appear on short stems in late spring, then keep the crown from sitting wet over winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant divisions in fall in a cool woodland spot.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a humus-rich, sharply drained woodland soil that stays cool at the root. This western species dislikes summer heat and heavy wet, so give dappled shade and good air movement. White petals twist around a white pouch in late spring, carrying a light fragrance.

planting.note: Set out dormant rhizomes in autumn for spring emergence.

directions: Plant the rhizome an inch below a gritty, humus-laden mix with excellent drainage and even spring moisture. This Asian species produces large inflated pouches in shades of rose to deep magenta in late spring. Give it a cold winter dormancy and light shade, and avoid disturbing the roots once settled.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in a cool, airy bed.

directions: Set the rhizome just under the surface in a very gritty, free-draining alpine-style soil that never waterlogs. This high-elevation species carries large, dusky maroon-purple pouches with a netted texture in late spring. Provide cool roots, bright dappled light, and a dry, cold winter rest.

planting.note: Plant dormant divisions in autumn or very early spring.

directions: This garden-raised hybrid is more forgiving than the species, wanting a humus-rich yet gritty soil in light shade. Plant the rhizome shallowly and keep evenly moist during growth without letting the crown stay soggy. Slender-petaled flowers with a soft pouch open in late spring and clumps bulk up steadily over the years.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn in a moist, seepy spot.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a constantly moist, seep-like soil that still drains freely at the root. This robust species can carry many small white-pouched, brown-sepaled flowers along a tall stem in late spring. Give it running moisture and light shade, and let it form large multi-stemmed colonies over time.

planting.note: Plant dormant rhizomes in autumn where winters are reliably cold.

directions: Set the slender creeping rhizome shallowly in a cool, humus-rich soil and give a deep, cold winter dormancy. This dwarf circumboreal species bears a single small pouch boldly blotched with rose-purple on white in late spring. It spreads by wandering runners, so allow room and avoid root disturbance.

planting.note: Pot up dormant pseudobulbs in late winter so flowers open with spring.

directions: Nestle the pseudobulb in an open, bark-based mix with only its lower third buried and the tip exposed. Flowers of rosy lilac with a fringed, spotted lip open in early spring, usually before the leaf expands. Water sparingly at first, increase as growth advances, then dry off completely for a cool winter rest.

planting.note: Start dormant pseudobulbs in late winter under cool glass.

directions: Plant the pseudobulb shallowly in a gritty, bark-rich medium with the top left proud of the surface. This is one of the few clear yellow species, with a brown-marked lip, blooming in early spring. Keep just moist while rooting, water freely in leaf, and rest bone-dry and cool through winter.

planting.note: Pot dormant pseudobulbs in late winter for a spring display.

directions: This popular, vigorous hybrid is among the easiest Pleione for a cool porch or alpine house. Seat the pseudobulb with its tip exposed in a free-draining bark mix and water lightly until roots form. Large pink flowers with a paler, boldly marked lip open in spring; dry the bulbs off for winter dormancy.

planting.note: Plant dormant pseudobulbs in late winter; hardy enough for a sheltered raised bed.

directions: One of the hardiest Pleione, this species can be grown outdoors in a gritty, sheltered pocket with sharp drainage. Set pseudobulbs shallowly and expect deep rose-pink flowers with a crimson-spotted lip in spring. Keep moist during growth and give a cold but dry winter rest; it clumps up freely.

planting.note: Pot dormant pseudobulbs in spring; this species flowers in autumn.

directions: Unusual among cultivated Pleione, this species blooms in autumn as the season cools. Plant the pseudobulb shallowly in an open bark mix, grow it on through summer, and expect rose-pink flowers with a white, fringed lip in fall. Rest the dormant bulbs dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in dappled woodland shade.

directions: Plant the crown a couple of inches deep in ordinary humus-rich soil in shade to part shade. This adaptable woodland orchid sends up leafy stems topped with many small greenish to dull purple flowers in mid to late summer. It tolerates a wide range of conditions once established and needs little fuss.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in autumn in a limy, gritty spot.

directions: Give this species a well-drained, alkaline soil with plenty of grit or limestone chippings. Plant the crown a couple of inches deep in sun to light shade. Dusky wine-red, vanilla-scented flowers line the stem in summer; keep it on the drier side and avoid rich, wet ground.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil.

directions: This robust garden hybrid stands out for its deep bronze-purple foliage and dusky flowers. Plant the running rhizome a couple of inches deep in rich, reliably moist soil in part shade. It spreads to form a handsome clump and blooms through summer; keep it well watered and divide when congested.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist ground.

directions: This vigorous selection thrives in wet, boggy soil or even the shallow edge of a pond in sun to part shade. Plant the fleshy roots shallowly and keep the soil constantly damp. Spiraled spikes of white, sweetly vanilla-scented flowers rise in autumn; it colonizes readily by runners.

planting.note: Plant dormant tuberous roots in autumn in a lean, well-drained spot.

directions: Unlike its bog-loving relatives, this species wants a short, dry, well-drained turf or gritty bed in full sun. Plant the small tuberous roots shallowly and avoid rich or wet soil. Delicate spiral spikes of tiny white flowers appear in late summer to autumn, above a rosette that forms after flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in damp, sunny ground.

directions: Plant the roots shallowly in a moisture-retentive soil in full sun to light shade. This species is notable for its pink to rose spiraled flower spikes rather than white, opening in summer. Keep the soil damp throughout growth; it will seed and spread in a congenial moist spot.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, shaded woodland soil.

directions: This evergreen Japanese species wants a humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade with shelter from cold wind. Plant the pseudobulbs so the base sits just below the surface and keep evenly moist. Bold spikes of clear golden-yellow flowers open in spring above broad pleated leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, humus-rich bed.

directions: A classic Japanese woodland orchid, this species takes a cool, shaded spot in leafy, free-draining soil. Set the pseudobulbs shallowly and keep the root zone evenly moist but never waterlogged. Spring spikes carry flowers with chocolate-brown sepals and a contrasting pale pink lip.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, moist woodland conditions.

directions: This deciduous species enjoys a humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in dappled to full shade. Plant the pseudobulbs just under the surface and keep them cool and evenly moist through the growing season. Late-spring spikes show yellow-green sepals framing a strongly frilled, red-brown lip.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, shaded soil.

directions: This yellow-flowered Japanese species wants leafy, free-draining soil in a shaded, sheltered position. Plant the pseudobulbs shallowly and keep the soil consistently damp during growth. Spring spikes carry warm golden-yellow flowers, often faintly veined, above the pleated foliage.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in spring in a moist, peaty medium.

directions: Plant the small tubers about an inch deep in a moisture-retentive, acidic mix of peat or sphagnum with sand. Keep constantly damp through the growing season, standing pots in a saucer of water in summer. Its famous flowers, resembling a white egret in flight with a deeply fringed lip, open in late summer; dry the tubers cooler for winter dormancy.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in autumn in a lean, well-drained meadow soil.

directions: This meadow orchid wants a short, unfertilized grassy sward or gritty bed in full sun to light shade. Plant the rounded tubers a couple of inches deep in autumn in soil that drains well and dries in summer. Purple to pink hooded flowers with green-veined side sepals open in late spring; leave the grass unmown until seed sets.

planting.note: Pot dormant tubers in early spring in a gritty alpine mix.

directions: This dainty Japanese alpine is usually grown in a pot of very free-draining, gritty compost with added leaf mold. Plant the small tubers shallowly and water carefully, keeping the medium moist but never sodden. Clusters of rose-pink to purple flowers with a spotted, three-lobed lip open in early summer; rest the tubers dry and frost-free in winter.

planting.note: Set potted plants outdoors only after nights stay reliably above 55F, and bring them back in before autumn frost.

directions: Pot divisions or rooted crowns at their original soil depth in a coarse, well-drained aroid mix and give the wide-spreading rosette plenty of room. Keep the soil evenly moist through the growing season and feed monthly, letting the top inch dry between waterings in winter. Mature specimens eventually push a pale green-and-white spathe, but the plant is grown chiefly for its huge deeply cut leaves.

planting.note: Move container plants to a shaded patio after frost danger passes and return them indoors well before the first fall chill.

directions: Plant this self-heading, mounding selection at soil level in a rich, free-draining mix and place it in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch of soil dries and feed lightly during spring and summer for full, glossy lobed foliage. It rarely blooms in cultivation and is valued instead for its tidy, shrub-like habit.

planting.note: Grown almost exclusively as an indoor container plant; give it warmth above 60F year round.

directions: Root a node cutting in a chunky, airy mix and provide a moss pole for the climbing stems to grip. Keep it in bright indirect light to hold the crisp white variegation, and let the mix approach dryness before watering again. Wipe leaves clean and remove any fully white leaves that scorch to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Best kept as a warm indoor houseplant; summer patio time is optional in frost-free spells only.

directions: Propagate from a stem node and set it in a loose, bark-rich potting medium with a support to climb. Bright filtered light keeps the green-and-white marbling stable, while too little light fades the pattern. Allow the top of the mix to dry slightly, then water thoroughly and empty the saucer to avoid soggy roots.

planting.note: Keep as an indoor plant in warm, humid conditions; it resents temperatures below 60F.

directions: Set a rooted cutting in a well-aerated aroid mix and stake the upright, slower-climbing stem. Give it bright indirect light so the white streaks and pink stems stay vivid, and water once the surface soil dries. Feed lightly in the growing season and pinch off any pure-white foliage that begins to brown.

planting.note: Grow indoors or on a shaded patio only when nights stay above 55F.

directions: Root a node cutting in a chunky mix and provide a totem so the multi-lobed leaves can enlarge as the plant climbs. New foliage emerges ghostly pale then deepens to green, a shift favored by bright indirect light. Keep the medium lightly moist during active growth and reduce watering when growth slows in winter.

planting.note: A frost-tender vine best summered outdoors in shade and overwintered indoors.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting against a moss pole in a fast-draining aroid mix. The deeply five-lobed, glossy green leaves grow larger as the vine matures and climbs, so bright indirect light and steady humidity help. Water when the top inch dries and feed monthly through spring and summer.

planting.note: Reliable warm-room houseplant; avoid exposure below 55F.

directions: Pot this compact, self-heading hybrid in a rich, well-drained mix at its original depth. New leaves emerge deep burgundy-red and mature to glossy dark green, with the best color under bright indirect light. Let the top of the soil dry before watering and dust the broad leaves periodically to keep them shining.

planting.note: An easy warm-room houseplant intolerant of frost.

directions: Set the plant in a free-draining potting mix and place it in medium to bright indirect light. The broad, emerald leaves form a tidy self-heading rosette that needs no stake. Allow the top inch to dry between waterings and feed lightly during the growing season for steady new growth.

planting.note: Grow indoors in warmth; frost-tender and best above 55F.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid at soil level in a well-drained mix and give it bright, indirect light to bring out the warm coppery-orange new growth. Water once the top inch dries, then let it drain freely. Feed monthly in spring and summer and it will keep a low, bushy shape without support.

planting.note: A warm indoor plant; protect from any frost.

directions: Grow this self-heading hybrid in a rich, airy mix set at the original planting depth. Emerging leaves are bright reddish-orange and deepen to green as they age, most vivid in bright indirect light. Keep the mix lightly moist during growth and allow slightly drier conditions in the cooler months.

planting.note: Frost-tender species grown indoors with high humidity.

directions: Root a node cutting in a chunky, moisture-retentive aroid mix and offer a pole for the stem to climb. The thick, leathery, wrinkled leaves that give it its name develop best in bright indirect light and steady warmth. Keep humidity high, water when the top inch dries, and avoid cold drafts.

planting.note: Grown as a warm houseplant; tolerates brief coolness but not frost.

directions: Pot this self-heading species in a very free-draining mix, since its swollen, water-storing leaf stalks make it sensitive to sogginess. Give it bright indirect light and let the mix dry out well between waterings. The glossy paddle-shaped leaves radiate from a short crown, and clumps can be divided to make new plants.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber; keep indoors in warmth or on a summer patio.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting against a moss pole in a bark-rich, well-drained mix. Juvenile leaves have a striking silvery-blue metallic sheen that gives way to larger arrow-shaped foliage as the vine climbs. Provide bright indirect light, water when the surface dries, and feed monthly during the growing season.

planting.note: Slow-growing frost-tender hybrid best kept warm indoors.

directions: Root a node cutting in a chunky, airy medium and provide a totem for the slow-climbing stem. The serrated leaves emerge in shades of cream, orange and green, and bright indirect light keeps the variegation strong. Let the mix dry partway down before watering and be patient, as this hybrid grows deliberately.

planting.note: Frost-tender foliage hybrid grown as an indoor plant.

directions: Grow this hybrid in a rich, well-drained aroid mix in bright indirect light. Its long, narrow, saw-toothed leaves give a bold textured look and hold color best out of direct sun. Water when the top inch dries, feed lightly in spring and summer, and keep it away from cold windows.

planting.note: Vigorous frost-tender climber for warm interiors or shaded summer patios.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in a free-draining mix and train the fast-growing stems up a pole or let them trail. Bright indirect light keeps the foliage a vivid golden-chartreuse, while low light turns it plain green. Water when the top inch dries and pinch tips to encourage a fuller plant.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber grown indoors or outdoors only in frost-free areas.

directions: Root a stem cutting and set it against a support in a rich, well-drained mix. The large glossy, arrow-shaped leaves elongate as the plant climbs, and bright indirect light suits it best. Keep evenly moist through the growing season, easing off in winter, and feed monthly for lush growth.

planting.note: A frost-tender crawling species grown as a warm, humid houseplant.

directions: Grow this creeping species in a wide, shallow pot of chunky aroid mix so the horizontal rhizome has room to travel. The large heart-shaped leaves have a subtle quilted texture and rippled margins, shown off best in bright indirect light. Keep the medium lightly moist and divide the rhizome to propagate.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber requiring warmth and humidity indoors.

directions: Root a node cutting in an airy aroid mix and provide a moss pole for climbing. Juvenile heart-shaped leaves are heavily overlaid with silver between green veins, a look that intensifies with maturity and bright indirect light. Keep humidity high, water when the top inch dries, and avoid cold, dry drafts.

planting.note: Frost-tender collector species needing high humidity and warmth indoors.

directions: Set a rooted cutting against a sturdy pole in a coarse, well-drained aroid mix. The long, strap-like leaves with deeply rippled, pleated margins can reach impressive size in warm, humid, brightly lit conditions. Keep the mix evenly moist, never soggy, and maintain steady warmth for the best foliage.

planting.note: Frost-tender dwarf species grown as a warm houseplant.

directions: Root a cutting in a chunky mix and offer a small support as the plant matures. Juvenile leaves are heart-shaped, then mature into a three-lobed shape with a soft velvety sheen and coppery tones in bright indirect light. Keep it warm and lightly moist, and let the surface dry slightly between waterings.

planting.note: Frost-tender species grown indoors in warmth.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in a free-draining aroid mix and give it bright indirect light. Its finely dissected, thread-like leaflets twist and curl to create a delicate, airy look unlike most philodendrons. Water when the top inch dries and provide steady warmth, protecting the fragile foliage from strong direct sun.

planting.note: Frost-tender species with a dormant dry season; grow indoors in warmth.

directions: Grow this large species in a rich, well-drained mix in bright indirect light. Its enormous deeply divided, feathery leaves make a dramatic display during the growing season. It naturally goes dormant and may drop leaves in the dry period, so reduce watering then and resume when new growth appears.

planting.note: Frost-tender compact climber for warm, humid interiors.

directions: Root a node cutting in an airy aroid mix and offer a small totem to climb. The thick, glossy, rounded heart-shaped leaves stay compact and neat under bright indirect light. Keep humidity up, water once the top inch dries, and avoid overwatering the fleshy stems.

planting.note: Frost-tender variegated climber grown warm indoors.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting on a moss pole in a chunky, well-drained mix. Maturing leaves become elongated and dragon-clawed, often splashed with green-and-cream variegation that is strongest in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and feed lightly during the growing season.

planting.note: Frost-tender hybrid grown as a warm houseplant.

directions: Root a cutting in a well-drained mix and give it bright indirect light to bring out its coloring. New leaves emerge lime-yellow speckled with green and are held on pink-blushed stems, deepening as they age. Provide a support for the climbing stem, water when the surface dries, and keep it warm.

planting.note: Frost-tender climber for warm interiors or frost-free gardens.

directions: Set a rooted cutting against a pole in a bark-rich, free-draining mix. As the vine climbs, its glossy leaves become increasingly deeply lobed with an oak-like outline. Give it bright indirect light, water when the top inch dries, and feed monthly through the warm season.

planting.note: Frost-tender velvety hybrid needing warmth and humidity indoors.

directions: This cross between verrucosum and melanochrysum grows best on a moss pole in a chunky, humid-tolerant mix. Its large velvety, dark green leaves with pale veins enlarge as the plant climbs in bright indirect light. Keep humidity high, water when the top inch dries, and shield the matte foliage from direct sun.
